title: HVAC Safety Hazards, Emergency Procedures, and Professional Boundaries
intended_use: Safety-aware HVAC guidance for homeowners, contractors, builders, and architects. Prioritizes life-safety over troubleshooting.

disclaimer.general: This knowledge file provides practical safety guidance, not a substitute for local code, manufacturer instructions, or professional diagnosis. If there is any doubt about safety, stop and contact qualified help.
disclaimer.assistant_behavior: When a life-safety trigger is detected, the assistant must interrupt troubleshooting, issue a clear safety callout, and direct the user to appropriate emergency or licensed services. Never provide DIY steps that increase risk.

severity_levels.immediate_emergency: Leave the house and/or call 911 immediately. Do not attempt troubleshooting.
severity_levels.serious_hazard: Stop work and call a professional immediately. Do not operate equipment until inspected.
severity_levels.diy_safe: Tasks a homeowner can do with basic tools and precautions (power off where applicable).
severity_levels.professional_only: Tasks requiring licensing, certification, specialized tools, training, or code compliance.

trigger_signs: Smell of gas or 'rotten eggs' (mercaptan odorant)
Hissing sound near gas meter, gas line, regulator, or appliance
Visible damage to gas piping, meter, or appliance connectors
Pilot light repeatedly going out combined with gas odor
Dead vegetation near buried gas lines or unexplained bubbling in standing water
Physical symptoms: dizziness, nausea, headache, eye irritation
immediate_response: Evacuate all occupants and pets immediately.
Leave the door open if possible as you exit.
Do NOT use telephones or cell phones inside.
Do NOT turn light switches on or off.
Do NOT light matches or lighters.
Do NOT start vehicles near the home.
Call 911 or gas utility from outside or neighbor's location.

trigger_signs: Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea, confusion, weakness, blurred vision
Symptoms improve when leaving the house
Soot streaks around furnace or draft hood
Yellow or flickering burner flame (should be steady blue)
immediate_response: Move everyone to fresh air immediately (outdoors).
Do not re-enter until cleared by emergency responders.
Do not ignore alarm even if you feel fine.
Do not run furnace to 'see if it goes away.'
who_to_call: Licensed HVAC technician for inspection after clearance

trigger_signs: Visible flames or smoke from equipment
Acrid smell of burning plastic or insulation
Popping, crackling, arcing, or buzzing sounds
immediate_response: Evacuate immediately if flames or heavy smoke.
Cut main power at breaker ONLY if safe and far from fire.
Use ABC or Class C extinguisher only if trained and fire is small.
DO NOT use water on electrical fire.
DO NOT stay to fight a large fire.

cracked_heat_exchanger.action: SHUT DOWN UNIT IMMEDIATELY - CALL PRO
signs: CO detector alerts or elevated readings
Sooting around furnace, burner rollout
Flame flicker/changes when blower starts
Unexplained headaches or nausea when heat runs
Rattling or popping noises during heat cycles
Visible rust or corrosion near heat exchanger
Fishy, acrid, or burning plastic smell
Smell gets stronger when unit runs
Warm/hot electrical covers
Discolored wiring or scorch marks

refrigerant_leak_enclosed_space.action: VENTILATE - EVACUATE IF SYMPTOMS - CALL PRO
signs: Hissing near coil/lineset with oily residue
Chemical odor, dizziness, headache in small room
System icing/frosting with poor cooling
Sudden loss of cooling performance
Standing water around indoor or outdoor unit
Water inside equipment cabinet
Wet control boards or electrical components
flooded_equipment.risk: Electrocution, short circuits, equipment often requires replacement

signs: Visible flashes inside cabinet
Loud popping or buzzing sounds
Breaker trips immediately upon reset

homeowners_must_never_do.reason: Risk of lethal electric shock (120V/240V). Moving parts can cause amputation.
Federal Law (EPA Section 608) requires certification. Fines up to $44,539 per day. Frostbite and asphyxiation risk.
Explosion risk. Requires specialized pressure testing and licensing.
Arc flash risk. Electrocution risk from main lugs even if main breaker is off.
Capacitors store lethal voltage even when power is off. Requires proper discharge procedure.
Requires specialized tools (combustion analyzer, camera) and training. Misdiagnosis can cause CO poisoning.
Never jump out limits, rollouts, or pressure switches. They trip for a reason (fire/safety).

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Turn thermostat off. Remove old filter. Insert new filter with airflow arrow pointing toward furnace/air handler.
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Filter slot shows scorching or melting, burning smell, repeated shutdowns

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Remove faceplate. Replace batteries with correct type. Check for corrosion.
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Corrosion or heat damage on wiring, system still unresponsive

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Turn system off. Keep 2 feet clearance. Remove leaves/twigs gently. Rinse with hose (power off).
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Unit buzzing or smoking, exposed wiring, oil stains, hissing sounds

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Look for tripped switches. Flip to OFF then ON. If trips again, STOP.
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Burning smell at panel, repeated tripping, sparking or buzzing

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Power off. Pour 1 cup distilled vinegar into drain line cleanout port to prevent algae.
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Standing water near electrical, repeated clogging, mold growth

homeowners_can_do.procedure: Ensure supply and return vents not blocked by furniture or rugs.
homeowners_can_do.stop_if: Whistling sounds, ice buildup, burning smell

professional_licensing.epa_608_certification: Required by federal law to handle refrigerants. Homeowners cannot legally add or recover refrigerant.

carbon_monoxide_detectors.placement: Every level, outside sleeping areas, 5-15 feet from fuel-burning appliances
carbon_monoxide_detectors.maintenance: Test monthly. Replace batteries annually. Replace unit every 5-7 years.

smoke_detectors.placement: Inside and outside every bedroom, on every level
smoke_detectors.maintenance: Test monthly. Replace per manufacturer guidance.

fire_extinguisher.location: Kitchen, garage, utility room near furnace
fire_extinguisher.usage: PASS: Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep. Only for small fires with clear exit.

gas_leak_detector.type: Handheld combustible gas sniffer or plug-in alarm
gas_leak_detector.note: Supplement to nose detection (rotten egg smell is primary warning)
